text.title: Launch Retro Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every launch retro must enforce. These keep the retro marketing-focused, data-anchored, and forward-actionable.

rule.statement: The retro covers five marketing sections: audience reaction, messaging fit, creative performance, channel ROI, lessons. Reject retros that collapse into engineering bug retrospectives — those belong in a separate incident retro.
rule.rationale: Mixed retros over-index on operational bugs (concrete) and under-discuss marketing dimensions (subjective). Separating them lets each function actually learn.

rule.statement: Each section opens with a data pull: numbers BEFORE narrative. Reject sections where impressions are unstated and "I felt like" leads the discussion.
rule.rationale: Anecdote-first retros produce shared mythology, not improvement. Data-first retros produce calibrated learnings.

rule.statement: Use blameless language: "the messaging didn't resonate with segment X" not "Marketing failed at messaging." Reject retros with finger-pointing or post-hoc blame assignment.
rule.rationale: Blame triggers defensiveness and stops information flow. Blameless retros surface 3-5× more actionable learning.

rule.statement: Every section produces explicit keep / change / drop entries. Reject vague "we should think about X" outputs — every learning is bucketed.
rule.rationale: Three-bucket categorization forces decisions; "we should think about" is a procrastination tag that never converts to action.

rule.statement: Each retro commits to exactly ONE change for the next launch, with three named owners (driver, reviewer, deadline-keeper). Reject retros that commit to 5+ changes (none gets done) or zero (nothing applied).
rule.rationale: One committed change converts at ~60%; five committed changes convert at ~5%. Constraining to one preserves the learning compound.

example.title: Data-first retro section
example.code:
## Channel ROI
Data: PH: listing #4 of day, 1,140 upvotes, 320 signups (8.6% click-to-signup) Twitter: 38 tweets, 1.2M impressions, 480 signups (cost: $0 organic) Email: 38k sent, 19% open, 4% click → 412 signups Podcast: 2 episodes, ~12k listens, ~80 signups (attribution rough)
Keep: Twitter cohort posting — outperformed expectation by 2x Change: Move PH posting from 7am PT to 6am PT — next-day data showed 6-7am as the highest-conversion window for the same listing tier Drop: Podcast cross-promo — attribution unclear AND cost was 14h prep for ~80 signups. ROI doesn't justify the slot next launch.
example.note: Illustrates r1, r2, r4 in a marketing-anchored section.

example.title: Vibes-first finger-pointing retro
example.code: "Honestly, marketing just didn't bring it. We should have hyped more. The email blast was weak — Bob should have written better subject lines. Next time we'll just do better."
example.note: Illustrates r2, r3, r4, r5 violations in two sentences. The retro produces zero actionable learning.
